hero.title: From clutter to clarity.
hero.sub: I help people make sense of complexity — through structure, rhythm and quiet decisions.

card.name: Zhao Yuanxuan · 赵元轩
card.name_body: Senior product designer working across data, decision systems and AI-native interfaces — 10+ years turning ambiguity into structure.
card.status_body: Open to teams building meaningful tools where design judgement and engineering rigor meet on equal terms.
card.based_body: Based in Beijing, working with global teams on long-horizon design — calm pace, sharp questions, lasting systems.

about.heading: About
about.sub: 10+ years across platforms and startups — designing systems for complexity, and exploring interaction in the AI era.
about.bio: 10+ years of digital product experience design — across large platforms and high-growth startups. I design systems that help people understand complexity, and explore interaction patterns for the AI era.

kuaishou.b1: Led UX design for HR and Organizational Development ToB/ToC products, while defining and guiding design standards.
kuaishou.b2: Led interaction design for innovative AI and low-code platform projects within the HR business.

lingdong.b1: Responsible for UX design of Lingdong Smart Home products.

tencent.b1: Led interaction design for Tencent Maps baseline and navigation modules; built and improved the component-library design principles from 0 to 1.

iqiyi.b1: Led design optimization and strategy for multi-platform products (mobile, PC, TV) and vertical modules of the overseas business division.

sf.b1: Responsible for ToC and ToB experiences across users, merchants, riders, rider management, and operations systems.
sf.b2: Built and scaled the interaction design team from 0–1–100, empowering business growth through design innovation.

beliefs.heading: Design Beliefs
items: In the AI era, the core of design is judgment and taste.
Great experience design, at its heart, lowers cognitive friction.

work.heading: Selected Work
work.sub: A short list of projects that defined how I think — across AI, data, mobility, and platform systems.

p1.title: AI Coding Assessment System

p2.title: Organizational Decision System Optimization
p2.line: Helping managers evaluate team performance distribution and make fairer decisions more efficiently.

p3.title: Chart Selection Engine

p4.title: Fulfillment Experience Governance

p5.title: Map Component System

p6.title: Immersive Viewing Experience

p7.title: Design Workflow System

contact.heading: Contact
contact.sub: For collaborations, work inquiries, or quiet conversations.

case.engine_pdf_fallback: Your browser cannot preview this PDF inline. Please use Open in the upper-right corner.

thesis.title: Not a UI optimization · but a redesign of the organizational decision system
thesis.caption: Facing a talent-management system that gradually failed as the business scaled, I shifted the focus from UI polish back to information flow and decision flow: what managers truly need to see, understand, and decide.

outcome.scenarios_sub: Performance Review · Talent Calibration · Promotion Review
outcome.ia_sub: Shared hierarchies, status language, and feedback rules across scenarios

diagnosis.title: The three major issues of workflow-driven systems
diagnosis.caption: Interface complexity is only the surface. The real issue: as the organisation scaled, its information flow and decision flow quietly stopped working.

cells.head: Critical signals drowning in low-value content
cells.body: Inconsistent page structures; information scattered across modules and tables; similar scenarios expressed differently — completing a single performance call required constant cross-page switching, comparison and memorisation.
cells.sym: Symptom · cross-page comparison · high memory load

cells.head: The organisation's information flow / decision flow begin to fail
cells.body: The visible problem is operational cost; the deeper one is that the system lacks an information architecture and behavioural logic built for organisational decisions. More UI polish only delays the collapse — it doesn't answer the systemic question.
cells.sym: Symptom · decisions without basis · invisible bias

cells.head: Behavioural uncertainty in complex systems
cells.body: "What does this filter affect?" "What does the current state mean?" "What should I do next?" — these once-fuzzy questions kept managers losing their bearings inside dense information.

reframe.title: From a workflow-driven system → an organisational decision platform
reframe.caption: Stop stacking historical processes on top of each other; reverse-engineer the system from business goals.
reframe.before_head: The system is a collection of features
before_list: Pages and tables stacked by legacy feature inheritance
Same information duplicated in many places, with inconsistent definitions
State vocabulary and rules invented module-by-module
Critical signals buried two or three pages deep
No way to support an organisation-wide judgment
reframe.after_head: The system carries organisational cognition · standards · decision logic
after_list: Information organised around organisational calibration / risk / performance / talent development
Unified information hierarchy, state vocabulary and interaction grammar
Views reorganised around "people" and "org" — not around "forms"
Result distribution, risk flags, historical trends and rater bias surfaced up front
Performance / inventory / promotion share the same design foundation

workflow.title: The fourth-generation evaluation workflow · 5 phases · end-to-end
workflow.caption: Performance review is restructured from a "task list + table" into a clear cadence: Preparation → Evaluation → Submit & calibrate → Done. Every phase has a clear role, density level, and key decision.

phases.name: Preparation
list: Result requirements / candidate list / employee gaps
BP view: warm-up and shared-context sync
A unified entry · see scope in one glance

phases.name: Evaluation
list: Manager view: peer · distribution · outliers · calibration
Judgment completed on a single screen — no more page-hopping

boards.title: Two-board redesign · structure / table remade together
boards.caption: Two boards capture the before/after of "page structure" and "core table" — one answers "where to look", the other answers "what to look at".
boards.media_tag: PDF · full-res original · click to open

boards.name: Overall page structure · before / after
boards.sub: What changed, and at what risk — the skeleton is reorganised around three decision pivots: project progress, cohort filtering, and outlier priority.
notes: Top-bar promotes real progress · raises priority and relevance of outlier alerts
Cohort filtering merged with target-distribution results
Detailed table collapsed and de-emphasised — evaluate whether this hurts decisions
Is cohort filtering now too hidden? · could merging fields affect sort behaviour?

boards.name: Core table · before / after
boards.sub: Trim columns · surface profile content · expose scoring in-line · hover reveals richer info. Let the "profile" absorb the detail; let the "main table" carry group-level judgment.
boards.alt: Table restructure & profile page design board
notes: 1 · Trim columns · merge & delete — keep only what decisions actually need
2 · Surface profile content — main table scans, profile carries detail
3 · Expose scoring in-line · merge exception info — judgment completed in one row
4 · Hover reveals richer info — lazy load · control the signal-to-noise ratio

principles.title: Seven cross-cutting design principles
principles.caption: Not local UX tweaks — system-wide constraints that hold across performance / inventory / promotion, and stay valid as the business expands.

items.head: Reorganise information around "people" and "org"
items.body: Not around forms and modules. Whichever flow they enter, the same person / org always presents the same unified view.

items.head: Raise signal-to-noise — don't show more
items.body: Shift the design goal from "complete the field set" to "surface what matters" — distribution, risk flags, history, rater bias.

items.head: Progressive disclosure · the critical task first
items.body: Main table scans group-level outliers / profile absorbs detail lookup / hover provides light, incremental info.

items.head: Consistent feedback language
items.body: What does this filter affect, what does the current state mean, what comes next — every scenario speaks the same feedback grammar.

items.head: Outliers first · sort by decision relevance
items.body: Pull "differences that need attention" from the middle of the table to the top — let managers see risk on first glance.

items.head: From individual scoring → organisational calibration
items.body: Calibration isn't a result view — it's the organisation aligning definitions and surfacing bias. Modelled as its own workflow node.

items.head: Design for sustainable evolution
items.body: The same hierarchy · state · feedback rules carry across performance / inventory / promotion — no need to redesign from scratch when business expands.

key_result.title: Chart-selection accuracy 89% · 74% higher than general AI
key_result.caption: Blind-test results across 200 real data requests (n = 200), comparing generic AI with this decision engine.

before_notes: Edge cases get shoehorned into a pie or bar.
Ignores unit consistency and label length.
after_notes: Intent-first → butterfly ↔ dumbbell are no longer mixed up.
STEP 1 catches Funnel / Sankey / Gantt / Chord up front.
Units, labels and domain signals are all encoded as rules.

panel.family_foot: The largest gains appeared in the Flow category (Funnel / Sankey / Gantt / Chord), where generic AI often forces pie or bar charts.
panel.version_foot: Chart coverage expanded from 31 → 47, while Top-1 accuracy improved from 71% → 89%.

pipeline.title: Six-layer Decision Pipeline · From Intent to Rendering
pipeline.caption: Click any layer to explore its responsibility and output within the decision chain.

sandbox.title: Chart Sandbox · Run a Decision
sandbox.caption: Choose an intent and data structure; the engine recommends primary and alternative charts with explanations.
sandbox.default_reason: Select an intent and data structure first.

atlas.title: 47-Chart Atlas · Grouped by Intent
atlas.caption: Switch between chart families; each tile represents the minimal symbol of a chart.

2.title: From a “workflow system” → to an organizational decision system
2.caption: As the talent-management system became ineffective with scale, I shifted the focus from UI to information and decision flow.
2.divider: P.04 · Design Boards · Talent Evaluation · Dual Proposal Document
2.center_name: From feature collection to a unified decision platform
2.center_hint: Let the system carry not only workflows, but also organizational cognition, management standards, and decision logic.

axes.name: Problem
axes.title: Three pathologies of a process-driven system
body: Inconsistent page structures, information scattered across modules and tables, similar scenarios expressed in different ways — completing a single performance call required constant cross-page switching, comparison and memorisation.
Critical decision signals were drowning in a sea of low-value content. The cost wasn't just clicks — it was that the organisation's “information flow” and “decision flow” were quietly breaking down.

The real issue wasn't interface complexity; it was the absence of an information architecture and behavioural logic built for organisational decisions. More UI polish would only delay the collapse, not address the systemic question.

axes.name: Reframe
axes.title: Reverse-engineer the system from business goals — stop stacking process on top of process
body: Use the four core OD objectives — organisational calibration, risk identification, performance judgment and talent development — as the basis for organising information, rather than inheriting the legacy process map.
Re-thread performance review, talent inventory and team calibration into a unified information hierarchy, state vocabulary and interaction grammar.
Transform a “collection of features” — a fragmented set of SaaS modules — into a “unified decision platform” where performance, inventory and promotion share the same design foundation.

axes.name: Architecture
axes.title: Lift the signal-to-noise ratio — re-organise information around “people” and “org”
body: The goal isn't to show more information, it's to raise its signal-to-noise ratio: result distribution, risk flags, historical trends and rater bias all surface first.
Once-fragmented table data is regrouped into unified views centred on “people” and “org” — collapsing the cost of jumping between flows to understand a single case.
Aggregation, layering and visualisation let managers form judgments fast: profile pages absorb the detail, while the main views surface group distribution and outliers.

axes.name: Behaviour
axes.title: Cut uncertainty — progressive disclosure and consistent feedback
body: Confront the behavioural uncertainty inherent to complex systems: what does a filter affect, what does the current state mean, what's the right next step — all re-designed as one consistent system-feedback loop.
Progressive disclosure: let users tackle the critical task first, then descend into detail — so they're never interrupted by information they don't yet need.
Reduce cognitive load in dense information environments, raise managerial throughput — and provide a stable framework for the talent system's continued evolution.

3.title: Tu·Ze — From intuition → to executable rules
3.caption: When facing a dataset, most designers choose charts by intuition. I spent six months abstracting that intuition into 47 executable rules, eventually building an AI Skill decision engine.
3.divider: P.03 · Decision Engine · Interaction Exploration · 47 Chart Types
3.center_hint: Start from the problem · answer with systems · implement with tools · iterate continuously.

axes.name: Problem
axes.title: A bad decision that keeps recurring — the cost of picking charts on instinct
body: The Bush v. Gore butterfly ballot — a single mis-designed form altered the outcome of a US presidential election. Proof that a visualisation mismatch is a design defect with real-world consequences.

Designers face three recurring binds every day: pie vs. bar with no standard to lean on, data structure that doesn't match the chart and gets bounced by engineering, and audience cognition that gets ignored — leaving the reader lost.
Hence the project's intent: not to build another chart catalogue, but a systematic decision framework — one that's reusable, transmissible, and callable by tools.

axes.name: Framework
axes.title: The optimal intersection of four dimensions — picking a chart isn't picking a shape
body: Picking a chart isn't picking a shape — it's picking the optimal intersection of «analytical intent × data structure × audience × scenario».

Analytical goals fall into five families — composition, trend, distribution, relation, flow. Data structure (a dimension × measure matrix) decides what's physically drawable. Audience layering (decision-makers / operators / analysts) sets the ceiling on cognitive load. Scenario (describe fact / analyse / argue a point) defines the chart's functional boundary.
Research benchmarks include John Snow's cholera map, Florence Nightingale's rose chart, McKinsey's static storytelling and OurWorldInData's interactive exploration.

axes.name: Engine
axes.title: Decision tree v3.23.0 — turning expert intuition into reusable rules
body: A six-layer pipeline: INTENT layer → PRE pre-processing → STEP 1 special-structure shortcut → STEP 2 / 2b general structure → STEP 3 / 3b edge cases → render layer (primary + alt tabs).

Three core insights: ① The «intent declaration layer» makes “just show this” vs. “analyze the change” yield completely different charts (butterfly vs. dumbbell) — principle: under-analyse rather than over-claim. ② «Unit consistency first» — any difference ≥ 10× demands a dual axis or a split, so rate values don't get compressed into nothing. ③ «Label-length rules outrank category-count rules» — 4-6 categories no longer default to a donut, and long labels force a horizontal bar.
From v3.10.0's 31 charts to v3.23.0's 47, across 13 patch releases that kept sharpening the edge cases.

axes.name: Outcome
axes.title: From research to tool — validating accuracy with quantitative data
body: Output #1 — a Flourish interactive chart-picking guide that designers and non-experts can both pick up, with the decision path made visible.
Output #2 — an AI Skill integration that packages the decision tree as a callable module, supporting ECharts / Chart.js / SVG render targets.

Quantitative validation: on a 200-sample blind set of real data requests, Top-1 accuracy jumps from 51% (general-purpose AI) to 89% (+38pp, +74% relative); Top-3 reaches 97%; edge-case families (Funnel / Sankey / Gantt / Chord) climb from 32% to 86%.
Iteration curve: 13 versions (v3.10 → v3.23), chart coverage 31 → 47, accuracy 71% → 89% — a steady, gradual climb that demonstrates this is engineered methodology, not lucky intuition.

4.title: From “interface optimization” → to experience governance for fulfillment systems
4.caption: I do not see design merely as interface optimization or workflow beautification, but as an operational capability connecting users, riders, and platform efficiency.
4.divider: P.01 · PDF Document · SF Express City Full Project
4.center_hint: Use user perception as the entry point and platform efficiency as the core.

axes.name: Growth
axes.title: Business growth — design as a conversion engine
body: Address reuse, map-based location picking and value-added service recommendation move the needle on user conversion and ordering efficiency.
Touchpoint packaging, tipping incentives and templated marketing campaigns build a positive feedback loop between user growth and service quality.

axes.name: Efficiency
axes.title: Fulfillment efficiency — better decisions for riders in messy environments
body: On the rider side, interactions are restructured around three axes: task comprehension, exception handling, route efficiency.
Order-relationship visualisation, route planning and information tagging help riders make the right call fast — even under multi-task, multi-state pressure.

axes.name: Trust
axes.title: User trust — faster ordering, transparent delivery, stronger value perception
body: On the user side, experience work centres on three goals: speed up ordering, make delivery transparent, sharpen value perception.
Lower the cognitive cost and raise the certainty of every step — both an experience goal and a long-term trust asset the platform compounds with its users.

axes.name: Long-term value
axes.title: Long-term platform value — systemic governance and architectural upgrades
body: Drive design unification and architectural upgrades: a single brand language, codified components, visual expression for complex flows, and targeted remediation of high-frequency complaints.
Make design capability replicable, extensible and ready to scale across teams — a continuous supply of long-term platform value.

5.title: From “responding to requirements” → to platform capability building
5.caption: Build an experience system with long-term evolution capability within complex business ecosystems and high-frequency scenarios.
5.divider: P.02 · PDF Document · Tencent Maps Full Project
5.center_name: An experience system with long-term evolution capability
5.center_hint: Unify cognition · reduce comprehension cost · improve information trustworthiness · enhance consistency and collaboration.

axes.name: Coherence
axes.title: Unified cognition — the foundational methodology of a design system
body: Building the component library from 0 to 1: enumerate business scenarios, abstract design rules, govern component logic, align with engineering on implementation standards.
Turn fragmented, repeated, weakly-aligned interaction assets into a reusable, extensible, collaborative design infrastructure.

axes.name: Clarity
axes.title: Lower the cost of understanding — systematic coverage across many scenarios and states
body: Cover every business line, every scenario and every state with an exhaustive experience audit — so any product team can find the “right expression” in any state.
Raises in-team collaboration efficiency, and lowers design + engineering cost as the business scales.

axes.name: Trust
axes.title: Boost credibility — handing off from WeChat location, surfacing user feedback
body: Users are naturally sensitive to map accuracy and to how transparently results are processed. Starting from the cognitive path, scenario mental model and information-transfer efficiency, the relationship between user and platform was re-mapped from the ground up.
Match WeChat's native visual and interaction language, refine loading logic and in-flight feedback — reinforcing the perception of consistency within the Tencent ecosystem.

axes.name: System
axes.title: Consistency & collaboration — from local experience to platform governance
body: Simplify feedback states, surface the critical result and use scenario-specific expression — reducing the user's understanding burden inside complex flows.
Push design beyond local optimisation toward systemic governance, platform-wide collaboration and long-term product value.

6.title: From “usability” → to an immersive content-consumption experience
6.caption: In global and high-frequency content-consumption scenarios, design should reconnect user perception, payment decisions, and platform goals into one coherent rhythm.
6.divider: P.05 · PDF Document · iQIYI International Full Project
6.center_name: The perceptual order of immersive content consumption
6.center_hint: Let viewing rhythm, payment decisions, and business goals naturally connect within one journey.

axes.name: Frequency
axes.title: High-frequency behaviour — the silent UX of speed-up, fast-forward, and scrubbing
body: The truly high-frequency action while watching isn't “press play” — it's speed-up, fast-forward and scrubbing. These are the gestures that decide whether a piece of content gets finished or abandoned.
Refine all three layers in parallel: cognition (predictable gestures), interaction (restrained visual feedback), perception (information at the right moment) — so no action breaks immersion.

axes.name: Hierarchy
axes.title: Information hierarchy — governing the priority of tips and playback feedback
body: Players are loaded with passive information (tips, recommendations, prompts, onboarding) that breaks the viewing experience at the wrong moments. The answer isn't to remove them — it's to layer them, time them, and scope them by scenario.
Establish a “main-task / supporting / background” three-tier structure: core content is never occluded; supporting info appears only between user actions; background info is deferred or merged.

axes.name: Decision
axes.title: Decision psychology — sense of direction and trust cost in the payment journey
body: On the surface, VIP payment looks like “too many steps”. The deeper problem is a lost sense of direction, an opaque process, conflated concepts — after several hops, users no longer know what they're doing or where they are.
Pivot the framing from “fewer steps” to “less uncertainty”: at every step, the user knows what comes next, what they can return to, and what they will finally walk away with.

axes.name: Flow
axes.title: Re-architecting the flow — collapsing fragmented hops into one “become VIP” goal experience
body: Take a serial multi-hop flow once scattered across plan selection, card binding, payment method and risk verification — and reassemble it into a single, unmistakable “become VIP” goal experience.
Bring in payment memory and state persistence: cards, plan choices and in-progress state are remembered — so leaving and returning feels like “the same path”, not “starting over”.
Design's value isn't only “usable”. It's the way it builds a natural, low-friction, durable bridge between user expectation, content rhythm and the business goal.

7.title: From “designing pages” → to designing systems
7.caption: As businesses become long-term, complex, and multi-platform, the bottleneck is no longer delivery capability, but how design resources are allocated, reused, and accumulated.
7.divider: P.06 · PDF Document · Design Workflow System Full Project
7.center_hint: From experience-driven work → to a systematic methodology built on data, scenarios, and cognition.

axes.name: Priority
axes.title: Set priorities — invest where “high-value × shippable” intersect
body: Demand always outnumbers people. Use a value-vs-investment 2×2 (an ROI lens) to evaluate every request: is it worth doing, when, and to what depth.
Separate “must do”, “should do”, “could do”, “shouldn't do” — keep design capacity from being diluted by low-value work, and let limited investment actually move the business.

axes.name: Insight
axes.title: Surface the real need — find the core problem with the «people · supply · scene» frame
body: Shift from experience-driven to “data + scenario + cognition” as a triad: qualitative work (field visits, user interviews, follow-up calls, usability tests) + quantitative work (event tracking, conversion funnels, user profiles, satisfaction models) jointly drive understanding.

Audit every request with a “people · supply · scene” lens: why a user reaches for it, in what context, what they walk away with — and how the platform's experience strategy reshapes behaviour and business in turn.

axes.name: System
axes.title: Systemic leverage — solve it once, apply it everywhere
body: Make every design delivery reusable: abstract one-off decisions into rules, components, templates and design assets so the next project naturally inherits the gains of the previous one.

Build differentiated strategies for each business surface — neither reinvent the wheel nor force a single standard. Trade a “system skeleton + differentiated skin” for the balance between efficiency and extensibility.

axes.name: Measurement
axes.title: Design measurement — making design impact a number you can argue about
body: Build a measurement system for design impact — from usability metrics and task-completion rates, to critical conversions, satisfaction modelling and long-term retention. “Is the design any good?” stops being a subjective question.
Run a data-driven loop: ship → measure → review → optimise. Every decision has a comparable baseline; long-term value becomes visible inside the numbers.
A mature design system isn't a short-term efficiency win — it's an organisational capability accumulated over time.

thesis.title: Not just shipping an interview tool — redefining "algorithm grinding" as "human–AI collaborative problem solving"
thesis.caption: As AI tooling becomes table stakes, LeetCode-style interviews drift further from real engineering. Starting from Kuaishou social-hire as the MVP, this project frames "can the candidate orchestrate AI inside a real codebase" as an assessable, observable, replayable hiring infrastructure — spanning the ATS, the Luobo interview room, an in-house IDE, and the evaluation & replay surfaces.

outcome.role_sub: Recruiter · Interviewer · Candidate, permissions gated by round
outcome.layer_sub: IDE · AI orchestration · Observability · Eval · Console · Library

diagnosis.title: A triple failure mode of technical interviewing in the AI era
diagnosis.caption: Benchmarking HackerRank / Codility / Nowcoder / Meta clarified the real problem: adding an AI chat box is not the answer. Failure happens simultaneously at the problem, the signal, and the hiring-flow layers.

cells.head: LeetCode-style and real engineering have diverged
cells.body: Single-file, dependency-free, context-free puzzles get one-shotted by AI. They no longer separate candidates by judgement in a real multi-file codebase.

cells.head: "Can AI solve it" ≠ "can the human steer AI"
cells.body: Looking only at the final diff cannot answer the questions that matter: was the AI output verified before adoption? Were hallucinated APIs caught? Was the prompt structured? Interviewers see code, not decisions.

cells.head: The hiring loop was never modelled for "AI assessment"
cells.body: From recruiter scheduling → interviewer setting tasks → candidate async solve → next-round interviewer reading the IDE, the path crosses many roles, states and notifications. A single weak link breaks the assessment.

reframe.title: From "evaluating code" → "evaluating human–AI collaboration"
reframe.caption: Reframe the unit of evaluation from "the artefact" to the temporary collaboration system formed by candidate × AI. The signal we score is process, not answer.
before_list: Problems: single-file algorithms detached from real work
Environment: AI banned, assume the candidate codes bare-handed
Flow: candidate solos · interviewer only sees outcome
Data: a single score · not replayable
reframe.after_head: Assessment = how the answer was arrived at, with AI
after_list: Problems: multi-file real engineering with AI-traps
Environment: AI open · structured prompts enforced
Flow: assessment decoupled from interview · next round can read the IDE
Data: Timeline · Decision Points · fully replayable

workflow.title: End-to-end · 5 phases · 4 systems
workflow.caption: From the recruiter scheduling to the next-round interviewer reading the IDE, the path threads through ATS · Luobo · in-house IDE · notification centre. Each phase has explicit owners, triggers and a state machine.

phases.name: Schedule
list: ATS syncs Luobo · assessment record created
Default off · required · interviewer gated by rollout

phases.name: Pick a task
list: KIM notice → enter library, choose one task
No rollback after dispatch · two-way nudges if idle

phases.name: Candidate solves
list: No forced submit · auto-save and close on timeout

phases.name: Next-round review
list: New "AI coding" tab inside the interview room
Read-only IDE · reference / solution folders visible
Behaviour summary modal for live follow-up

arch.title: Six layers · from candidate to decision
arch.caption: More than an IDE embedded in Luobo. The system is sliced into 6 layers along "capture → orchestrate → observe → evaluate → present". Each layer owns its state machine and data contract.

items.name: Candidate Workspace · in-house IDE
items.cap: Open-source IDE embedded in Luobo · file tree / editor / terminal / AI panel · 60min timer · dual-camera monitoring

items.name: AI Orchestration · unified gateway
items.cap: All AI calls via one proxy · structured prompt enforcement · model routing · tool calls · context · memory

items.name: Observability · behaviour layer ⭐
items.cap: 4-lane Timeline · AI Interaction Graph · Decision Points · the single source of truth for evaluation · fully replayable

items.name: Evaluation Engine
items.cap: Rules + LLM-as-judge dual engines · 4-axis radar · every score links back to a Timeline event

items.name: Interviewer Console
items.cap: Multi-perspective replay · AI-drafted follow-ups · behaviour summary modal · next round can read the IDE (with reference folder)

items.name: Scenario & Task Library
items.cap: Real engineering tasks (multi-file · AI-traps) · AI-Solvability score · maintained in Luobo backend · tagged by team / role / language

surfaces.title: 7 core surfaces · the full experience from candidate to interviewer
surfaces.caption: The design lands as 7 role-specific surfaces — each screen carries a single intent, and the surfaces are stitched together by a shared visual language and the system state machine. Structural prototypes below.

items.name: Candidate Workspace · in-house IDE
items.sub: Open-source IDE embedded in Luobo: file tree on the left, editor in the middle, terminal on the bottom, AI panel on the right. A persistent 60-minute timer and dual-camera preview sit in the top bar.

items.name: AI Orchestration · unified gateway
items.sub: All AI requests pass through one proxy that enforces structured prompts, routes models and manages context. Shown here from the backend operator's perspective.

items.name: Observability · 4-lane Timeline
items.sub: Four lanes: Prompt · Code Diff · Terminal · Browser. Decision Points highlight moments of adopt / reject / verify. This is the single source of truth that feeds the evaluation engine.

items.name: Evaluation · 4-axis Radar
items.sub: Rules and LLM-as-judge jointly produce scores across the four axes. Every score drills down to a concrete event on the Timeline.

items.name: Interviewer Console · Replay
items.sub: Inside the interview room the next-round interviewer opens the AI Coding tab: behaviour summary card + read-only IDE + Decision Point jumps + AI-drafted follow-up questions.

items.name: Interviewer Dashboard · cross-candidate
items.sub: Cross-candidate view for interviewers and hiring managers: 4-axis distributions, flagged behaviour, container health and per-task AI-Solvability feedback.

items.name: Scenario & Tasks · library
items.sub: Interviewers pick a task: filters on the left (team / role / language / difficulty), README preview on the right. Each task is tagged with AI-Solvability and adoption rate.

dims.title: 4-axis rubric · every signal links back to an event
dims.caption: Rule engine handles hard signals (did they run tests, did they leak API keys); LLM-as-judge handles prompt quality and reasoning depth. Every score traces back to a specific timestamp on the Timeline.

items.name: Problem Decomposition
items.cap: Can they produce a subtask list within 3 minutes that covers edge cases?
items.sig: Signals: # subtasks / edge coverage / plan-first behaviour

items.name: AI Orchestration
items.cap: Are prompts structured? Is the right model routed? How deep do they iterate?
items.sig: Signals: prompt clarity · model choice · avg loop count

items.name: Verification
items.cap: Do they run tests before adopting AI output? Do they catch hallucinated APIs?
items.sig: Signals: unverified adoptions · hallucination catch-rate · rollback behaviour

items.name: Production Thinking
items.cap: Do they think about idempotency · monitoring · degradation · log redaction?
items.sig: Signals: test coverage delta · engineering defects · production discussion

metrics.title: MVP rollout targets · making results measurable for the business
metrics.caption: Beyond quantitative targets, the more important question is qualitative: "did the assessment actually influence hiring decisions?" — the single anchor of project value.

items.def: Interviews with AI Coding on / all interviews in rollout (the feature is being used)
Candidates who entered / candidates notified (candidate-side flow is smooth)
Active submits / candidates entered (difficulty and time are calibrated)
Interviewers opening the AI Coding tab / interviews with AI Coding (the result is trusted)
Interviewer survey: did AI Coding influence the hiring decision (qualitative · the single anchor)

principles.title: 7 system design principles
principles.caption: Not local UX rules but the boundaries this system must never trade away as it evolves.

items.head: Observe process, not outcome
items.body: Observability is the only source of truth for evaluation; every score must link back to a concrete Timeline event.

items.head: AI calls must be controllable
items.body: All AI requests pass through a unified proxy · structured prompts enforced · injection detection · hallucinated-API checks.

items.head: Tasks must resist one-shot solves
items.body: Every task carries an AI-Solvability score; the sweet spot is "AI can assist but human judgement is required".

items.head: Permissions gated by round
items.body: Only the next-round interviewer sees the IDE · reference folder is interviewer-only · candidate cannot re-enter.

items.head: Assessment decoupled from interview
items.body: Standalone assessment room · isolated from the interview room · containers and state self-managed · cancel cascades correctly.

items.head: Infrastructure is first-class
items.body: Container start-success · start-time · interruption-rate are first-class metrics — they decide whether the system can carry hiring trust.

items.head: Designed to evolve
items.body: MVP covers social-hire with one task, but IDE · evaluation · library protocols leave room for multi-task · campus hiring · auto-scoring from day one.
